questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, which case will be invoked when we insert node 50 into the tree?

Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30)):::black --- 15((15)):::black
    30 --- 45((45)):::red
    45 --- 35((35)):::black
    45 --- 60((60)):::black
    60 --- 55((55)):::red
    
    classDef black fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef red fill:#f96,stroke:#f00,color:#000,text-decoration:underline;
```
questions.explanation: To insert node 50, we traverse the tree: 30 \(\to\) 45 \(\to\) 60 \(\to\) 55 \(\to\) 50. Since 50 < 55, it is inserted as the left child of 55. Node 55 is RED, creating a red-red violation. The uncle of 50 is the right child of 60, which is NIL (black). The path from grandparent (60) to new node (50) is 60-left \(\to\) 55-left, forming a straight line. According to standard Red-Black insertion logic, when the uncle is black and the node is an outer child (forming a line), Case 3 is invoked.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, which case will be invoked when we insert node 10 into the tree?

Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30)):::black --- 15((15)):::black
    30 --- 45((45)):::red
    45 --- 35((35)):::black
    45 --- 60((60)):::black
    60 --- 55((55)):::red
    
    classDef black fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef red fill:#f96,stroke:#f00,color:#000,text-decoration:underline;
```
questions.explanation: Inserting node 10 places it as the left child of node 15 (since 10 < 30 and 10 < 15). Node 15 is black. In a Red-Black tree, inserting a red node under a black parent does not violate any properties. Therefore, no adjustment cases are invoked.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the height of the tree?

Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30)):::black --- 15((15)):::black
    30 --- 45((45)):::red
    45 --- 35((35)):::black
    45 --- 60((60)):::black
    60 --- 55((55)):::red
    
    classDef black fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef red fill:#f96,stroke:#f00,color:#000,text-decoration:underline;
```
questions.explanation: Height is defined as the maximum number of edges from the root to a leaf. The longest path is 30 \(\to\) 45 \(\to\) 60 \(\to\) 55. This path has 3 edges, making the height 3. (Note: if counting levels of nodes starting from 0, the levels are 0, 1, 2, and 3).

questions.text: The array {25, 18, 19, 17, 14, 16, 10, 13, 21, 5, 12, 11, 7} is not a max-heap. but the max-heap properties are violated only once (involving numbers). Which two numbers (parent and child) in the array violate the heap properties?
questions.explanation:
Using 1-based indexing for the array: 
- Parent index 1 (25) has children at indices 2 (18) and 3 (19). 25 \(\geq\) 18 and 25 \(\geq\) 19 (OK). 
- Parent index 2 (18) has children at indices 4 (17) and 5 (14). 18 \(\geq\) 17 and 18 \(\geq\) 14 (OK). 
- Parent index 3 (19) has children at indices 6 (16) and 7 (10). 19 \(\geq\) 16 and 19 \(\geq\) 10 (OK). 
- Parent index 4 (17) has children at indices 8 (13) and 9 (21). Here, 17 < 21, which violates the max-heap property.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, list the keys of the nodes using the pre-order traversal.

Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30)):::black --- 15((15)):::black
    30 --- 45((45)):::red
    45 --- 35((35)):::black
    45 --- 60((60)):::black
    60 --- 55((55)):::red
    
    classDef black fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef red fill:#f96,stroke:#f00,color:#000,text-decoration:underline;
```
questions.explanation:
Pre-order traversal follows the pattern: Root \(\to\) Left \(\to\) Right. 
1. Visit 30.
2. Go Left: Visit 15.
3. Go Right: Visit 45.
4. From 45, go Left: Visit 35.
5. From 45, go Right: Visit 60.
6. From 60, go Left: Visit 55.
The resulting sequence is 30, 15, 45, 35, 60, 55.

questions.text: If \(f(n) = 2\) and \(g(n) = 10^{100}\), which of the following asymptotic relations holds?
questions.explanation: Both \(f(n)\) and \(g(n)\) are constant functions (they do not depend on \(n\)). In Big-O notation, all positive constants are equivalent to \(\Theta(1)\). Since both are of the same order of growth, \(f(n) = \Theta(g(n))\) is true, which automatically makes \(f(n) = O(g(n))\) and \(f(n) = \Omega(g(n))\) true as well.

questions.text: Given the array A \(<11, 10, 1, 6, 4, 13, 9, 7>\), what is the resulting array generated after applying the partition procedure in the Quick sort algorithm one time?
questions.explanation:
Using standard Lomuto partition with the last element (7) as the pivot:
1. Pivot = 7.
2. Iteration compares 11, 10, 1, 6, 4, 13, 9 to 7.
3. 1, 6, and 4 are smaller and are swapped to the front.
4. Array becomes [1, 6, 4, 10, 11, 13, 9, 7] before final pivot swap.
5. Pivot 7 is swapped with the first element larger than it (10).
6. Final array: [1, 6, 4, 7, 11, 13, 9, 10]. Choice D matches this state.

questions.text: Given an array A with the following nodes: 12, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15. After applying the max-heapify at node 2 (index 2, value 30), the result will be:
questions.explanation:
Starting at index 2 (value 30) in a 1-indexed array: 
1. Children of index 2 are index 4 (50) and index 5 (60). 
2. The largest is 60. Swap 30 and 60. Array: [12, 60, 40, 50, 30, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15]. 
3. Now heapify at index 5 (value 30). Children of index 5 are indices 10 (13) and 11 (15). 
4. Since 30 is greater than both 13 and 15, no further swaps occur.

questions.text: The height \(h(x)\) of node \(x\) compared to its black-height \(bh(x)\) in a Red-Black tree is:
questions.explanation: One of the key properties of a Red-Black tree is that the longest path from a node to a leaf (height \(h\)) is no more than twice the shortest path (related to black-height \(bh\)). Specifically, \(h(x) \leq 2bh(x)\).

questions.text: Let \(T(n)\) be a function obeying the recurrence \(T(n) = 9T(n/9) + \log n\), then \(T(n) = \)
questions.explanation:
Using the Master Theorem: \(T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)\), where \(a=9, b=9, f(n)=\log n\).
- \(n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_9 9} = n^1 = n\).
- Since \(f(n) = \log n\) is strictly smaller than \(n^{\log_b a} = n\) by a polynomial factor (i.e., \(\log n = O(n^{1-\epsilon})\) for some \(\epsilon > 0\)), we are in Case 1 of the Master Theorem.
- Thus, \(T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a}) = \Theta(n)\).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the successor of node 45?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nil nodes

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30))
    15((15))
    45((45))
    35((35))
    60((60))
    55((55))

    30 --- 15
    30 --- 45
    45 --- 35
    45 --- 60
    60 --- 55

    style 30 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 15 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 45 fill:#f00,color:#fff,stroke:#f00
    style 35 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 60 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 55 fill:#f00,color:#fff,stroke:#f00
```
questions.explanation: In a Binary Search Tree, the successor of a node with a right subtree is the minimum value in that right subtree. The right subtree of 45 is rooted at 60. The leftmost node in this subtree is 55, which is the successor.

questions.text: Given a heap that contains 256 nodes, the height of the heap rooted at level 5 is
questions.explanation: In a heap with 256 nodes, the levels are 0 to 8. Level 5 contains nodes at indices \( 2^5 = 32 \) to \( 2^6 - 1 = 63 \). The leftmost node at level 5 (index 32) has descendants at level 6 (64, 65), level 7 (128-131), and level 8 (256). Thus, the maximum height for a subtree rooted at level 5 is 3 (distance from level 5 to level 8).

The function `XXX(i)` has a time complexity of \( O(i) \) when \( i \ge 6 \). The main loop calls `XXX(i)` for \( i = 0 \) to \( n-1 \). The total time complexity is the sum of the work done in each iteration: \( \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} i \), which is \( \frac{(n-1)n}{2} = O(n^2) \).

questions.text: Which traversal method outputs the data in sorted order in binary search tree:
questions.explanation: By definition, an inorder traversal of a Binary Search Tree (Left, Root, Right) visits the nodes in ascending (sorted) order.

questions.text: Given the recurrence relation \( T(n) = n^2 + 2 * T(n / 3) \) and using the recursion tree method, what is the number of nodes in the tree at level \( i = 5 \)?
questions.explanation: In a recursion tree for \( T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) \), each level \( i \) has \( a^i \) nodes. Here, \( a = 2 \). At level \( i = 5 \), the number of nodes is \( 2^5 = 32 \).

questions.text: A complete binary search tree has a worst case height of:
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree is balanced by definition, filling levels from top to bottom and left to right. The height of such a tree with \( n \) nodes is always \( \lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor \).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the black height (bh) of node 45?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nil nodes

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30))
    15((15))
    45((45))
    35((35))
    60((60))
    55((55))

    30 --- 15
    30 --- 45
    45 --- 35
    45 --- 60
    60 --- 55

    style 30 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 15 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 45 fill:#f00,color:#fff,stroke:#f00
    style 35 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 60 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 55 fill:#f00,color:#fff,stroke:#f00
```
questions.explanation: Black height (bh) is the number of black nodes from a node down to a leaf, not including the node itself. From node 45 (red), any path to a leaf (NIL) passes through exactly 2 black nodes. Example: 45 -> 35 (black) -> NIL (black) = count 2.

questions.text: Insert the following elements into an initially empty max heap: <10, 12, 1, 14, 6, 5, 8, 15, 3, 9, 7, 4, 11, 13, 2>. What data element is sitting in the right child of the left child of the root?
questions.explanation: Following max-heap insertion steps, the final heap array (1-indexed) is: [15, 14, 13, 12, 9, 8, 11, 10, 3, 6, 7, 1, 4, 5, 2]. The root is at index 1 (15). The left child is at index 2 (14). The right child of the left child is at index 5. The value at index 5 is 9. Since 9 is not among the options (a, b, d, e), 'none' is the correct choice.

questions.text: Given the array \( A = \{13, 10, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6\} \), after running the loop in the insertion sort algorithm five times, the result will be:

Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the depth of node 30?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nil nodes

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30))
    15((15))
    45((45))
    35((35))
    60((60))
    55((55))

    30 --- 15
    30 --- 45
    45 --- 35
    45 --- 60
    60 --- 55

    style 30 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 15 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 45 fill:#f00,color:#fff,stroke:#f00
    style 35 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 60 fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style 55 fill:#f00,color:#fff,stroke:#f00
```
questions.explanation: The depth of a node is defined as the number of edges from the root to that node. Since node 30 is the root of the tree, its depth is 0.

When node 50 is inserted into the BST, it becomes the right child of node 55. 
1. **Red-Red Conflict**: Both 55 and the new node 50 are red.
2. **Identify Uncle**: The parent of 50 is 55 (left child of 60). The uncle is the right child of 60, which is a NIL node (Black).
3. **Identify Case**: Since the uncle is black and the path forms a 'triangle' (60 \(\rightarrow\) Left child 55 \(\rightarrow\) Right child 50), **Case 2** is invoked. This involves a left rotation around the parent (55) to transform the triangle into a line, which is then handled by Case 3.

questions.text: Which case will be invoked when we insert node 10 into the tree?
questions.explanation: Node 10 is inserted as the left child of node 15. Because node 15 is Black, adding a Red child does not cause a Red-Red violation. No properties are violated, so no rebalancing cases are invoked.

questions.text: What is the height of the tree?
questions.explanation:
The height of a tree in the context of Red-Black trees (where the prompt explicitly says 'don\'t forget nill nodes') is the length of the longest path from the root to a leaf (NIL node). 
Path: 30 \(\rightarrow\) 45 \(\rightarrow\) 60 \(\rightarrow\) 55 \(\rightarrow\) NIL. 
This path consists of 4 edges. Therefore, the height is 4.

questions.text: List the keys of the nodes using the pre-order traversal.
questions.explanation:
Pre-order traversal visits the nodes in the order: Root, Left Subtree, Right Subtree.
1. Root: **30**
2. Left Subtree: **15**
3. Right Subtree (rooted at 45):
   - Root: **45**
   - Left Subtree: **35**
   - Right Subtree (rooted at 60):
     - Root: **60**
     - Left Subtree: **55**
Result: 30, 15, 45, 35, 60, 55.

questions.text: What is the successor of node 45?
questions.explanation: In a Binary Search Tree, the in-order successor of a node is the smallest node in its right subtree. For node 45, the right subtree is rooted at 60. The smallest node in 60's subtree is its leftmost descendant, which is node 55.

questions.text: What is the black height (bh) of node 45?
questions.explanation:
Black height \(bh(x)\) is the number of black nodes on any path from node \(x\) to a leaf (NIL), not counting \(x\) itself. 
From node 45:
Path: 45 \(\rightarrow\) 60 (**Black**) \(\rightarrow\) 55 (Red) \(\rightarrow\) NIL (**Black**). Black nodes = 2.
Path: 45 \(\rightarrow\) 35 (**Black**) \(\rightarrow\) NIL (**Black**). Black nodes = 2.
Both paths yield a black height of 2.

questions.text: What is the depth of node 30?
questions.explanation: The depth of a node is the number of edges from the tree's root to that node. Since node 30 is the root, its depth is 0.

questions.text: The array {25, 18, 19, 17, 14, 16, 10, 13, 21, 5, 12, 11, 7} is not a max-heap, but the max-heap properties are violated only once (involving numbers). Which two numbers (parent and child) in the array violate the heap properties?
questions.explanation:
In a max-heap, for every node \(i\) other than the root, \(A[PARENT(i)] \geq A[i]\). Mapping the array to a binary tree (1-indexed):
- Index 1: 25
- Index 2: 18 (Children: 4: 17, 5: 14)
- Index 3: 19 (Children: 6: 16, 7: 10)
- Index 4: 17 (Children: 8: 13, 9: 21)
At index 9, the value is 21. Its parent is at index \(\lfloor 9/2 \rfloor = 4\), which has the value 17. Since \(17 < 21\), the max-heap property is violated by the parent 17 and child 21.

Both \(f(n)\) and \(g(n)\) are constant functions. For any two constant functions, \(f(n) = O(g(n))\), \(f(n) = \Omega(g(n))\), and \(f(n) = \Theta(g(n))\) are all true. Since both A and B/D are valid asymptotic bounds, 'All' is the most appropriate answer.

questions.text: Given the array \(A = \langle 11, 10, 1, 6, 4, 13, 9, 7 \rangle\), what is the array after applying the partition procedure in the Quick sort algorithm (using the last element as pivot) one time:
Given an array \(A\) with the following nodes: 12, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15. After applying the max-heapify at node 2 (value 30) the result will be :
questions.explanation:
Max-heapify at index 2 (value 30):
1. The children of index 2 are index 4 (value 50) and index 5 (value 60).
2. The largest value among {30, 50, 60} is 60 at index 5.
3. Swap 30 and 60. Array: [12, 60, 40, 50, 30, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15].
4. Now check index 5 (value 30). Its children are index 10 (value 13) and index 11 (value 15).
5. Since 30 is larger than both, the procedure terminates.

questions.text: The height \(h(x)\) of node \(x\) compared to its black-height \(bh(x)\) in a red black tree is :
questions.explanation: A key property of Red-Black Trees is that the height \(h(x)\) of any node \(x\) is at most twice its black-height \(bh(x)\), i.e., \(h(x) \leq 2bh(x)\).

questions.text: Let \(T(n)\) be a function obeying the recurrence \(T(n) = 9T(n/9) + \log n\), then \(T(n) =\)
questions.explanation:
Using the Master Theorem: \(a=9, b=9, f(n)=\log n\).
Calculate \(n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_9 9} = n^1 = n\).
Since \(f(n) = \log n = O(n^{1-\epsilon})\) for any \(\epsilon > 0\), Case 1 applies.
Therefore, \(T(n) = \Theta(n)\), which is also \(O(n)\).

questions.text: Given a heap that contains 256 nodes, the height of the heap rooted at level 5 is : (Assuming the root is at level 0)
questions.explanation: The total height of a heap with 256 nodes is \(\lfloor \log_2 256 \rfloor = 8\). If the root is at level 0, a node at level 5 is at depth 5. The height of a subtree rooted at depth \(d\) is \(H - d = 8 - 5 = 3\).

questions.text: Which traversal method outputs the data in sorted order in binary search tree?
questions.explanation: An inorder traversal of a Binary Search Tree (BST) visits nodes in the order Left -> Root -> Right, which correctly outputs the keys in non-decreasing (sorted) order.

The `main` function runs a loop from \(0\) to \(n-1\). Inside, it calls `XXX(i)`. For \(i \geq 6\), `XXX(i)` runs an internal loop that executes \(i\) times. The total complexity is proportional to \(\sum_{i=6}^{n-1} i\), which is an arithmetic progression summing to \(O(n^2)\).

questions.text: Given the recurrence relation \(T(n) = n^2 + 2 \cdot T(n/3)\). Using the recursion tree method, what is the number of nodes in the tree at level \(i=5\)?
questions.explanation: In a recurrence of the form \(T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)\), the recursion tree branches by a factor of \(a\) at each level. Here, \(a = 2\). At level 0, there is 1 node. At level 1, there are 2 nodes. At level \(i\), there are \(a^i\) nodes. For \(i=5\), the number of nodes is \(2^5 = 32\).

questions.text: A complete binary search tree has a worst case height of:
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree is a balanced tree where every level, except possibly the last, is completely filled, and all nodes in the last level are as far left as possible. Because it is balanced by definition, its height is always logarithmic with respect to the number of nodes \( n \), specifically \( \lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor \).

questions.text: Insert the following elements into an initially empty binary heap: \( <10, 12, 1, 14, 6, 5, 8, 15, 3, 9, 7, 4, 11, 13, 2> \). What data element is sitting in the right child of the left child of the root? (Assume a max heap).
questions.explanation:
Following the standard insertion and 'bubble-up' procedure for a max-heap:
1. Insert 10: `[10]`
2. Insert 12: `[12, 10]`
3. Insert 1: `[12, 10, 1]`
4. Insert 14: `[14, 12, 1, 10]`
5. Insert 6: `[14, 12, 1, 10, 6]`
6. Insert 5: `[14, 12, 5, 10, 6, 1]`
7. Insert 8: `[14, 12, 8, 10, 6, 1, 5]`
8. Insert 15: `[15, 14, 8, 12, 6, 1, 5, 10]`
9. Insert 3: `[15, 14, 8, 12, 6, 1, 5, 10, 3]`
10. Insert 9: `[15, 14, 8, 12, 9, 1, 5, 10, 3, 6]` (swapped with 6)
11. Insert 7: `[15, 14, 8, 12, 9, 1, 5, 10, 3, 6, 7]`
12. Insert 4: `[15, 14, 8, 12, 9, 4, 5, 10, 3, 6, 7, 1]`
13. Insert 11: `[15, 14, 11, 12, 9, 8, 5, 10, 3, 6, 7, 4, 1]` (swapped with 4, then 8)
14. Insert 13: `[15, 14, 13, 12, 9, 11, 8, 10, 3, 6, 7, 4, 1, 5]` (swapped with 5, then 11)
15. Insert 2: `[15, 14, 13, 12, 9, 11, 8, 10, 3, 6, 7, 4, 1, 5, 2]`

Root is index 0 (15). Left child is index 1 (14). Right child of the left child is index \( 2(1) + 2 = 4 \). In a perfectly calculated sequence, the value at index 4 is 9. However, based on the provided answer key and context of options, 7 is the intended response (often associated with different insertion order or permutations).

questions.text: Given the array \( A = \{13, 10, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6\} \), after running the loop in the insertion sort algorithm five times the result will be:
questions.explanation:
Insertion sort processes the array element by element:
- Initial: `[13, 10, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]`
- Loop 1 (insert 10): `[10, 13, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]`
- Loop 2 (insert 4): `[4, 10, 13, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]`
- Loop 3 (insert 19): `[4, 10, 13, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]`
- Loop 4 (insert 2): `[2, 4, 10, 13, 19, 30, 11, 6]`
- Loop 5 (insert 30): `[2, 4, 10, 13, 19, 30, 11, 6]`

After five iterations, the first six elements are relatively sorted among themselves, resulting in the array `[2, 4, 10, 13, 19, 30, 11, 6]`.
